does: This paper introduces GAAP, an execution environment for AI agents that enforces user privacy through a permission-based access control system. The system collects privacy specifications from users via dynamic prompts that describe how their private data may be shared, then monitors and enforces agent behavior to ensure disclosed information complies with these specifications. Crucially, GAAP provides deterministic privacy guarantees without requiring the AI agent or model provider to be trusted with raw sensitive data, preventing exfiltration attacks including prompt injection attempts that could otherwise manipulate agents into revealing private information.
connects: This work builds on decades of research in secure computing, information flow control, and access control systems, adapting techniques like sandboxing and capability-based security to the novel challenge of AI agents handling sensitive user data. It responds directly to emerging concerns about prompt injection vulnerabilities and model extraction attacks highlighted in recent adversarial ML research, while extending traditional privacy frameworks beyond simple data minimization to dynamic permission enforcement. The approach combines insights from operating system security boundaries with policy-based access control mechanisms, addressing the specific problem that users must delegate agency to potentially untrustworthy AI systems.
enables: GAAP establishes foundations for building AI agents that can safely access and operate on personal data without requiring absolute trust in the model provider. This enables practical deployment of AI personal assistants for sensitive domains like healthcare, finance, and legal services where privacy is critical. The work points toward broader verification frameworks for AI agent behavior and could inspire formal methods for reasoning about privacy-preserving agent execution. It also opens questions about expressing granular privacy policies that users actually understand and can specify, and techniques for efficiently enforcing fine-grained privacy constraints at scale across diverse agent architectures and deployment models.
